[ACT (Action Chunking with Transformers)](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/main/en/act)<br/><br/># LeRobot: Hugging Face's Robotics Learning Framework<br/><br/>**LeRobot** is Hugging Face's open-source library for training robot policies using imitation learning, featuring standardized implementations of multiple policy architectures.[1]<br/><br/>## Key Policy Architectures<br/><br/>**ACT (Action Chunking with Transformers)**[1][5]<br/>- Transformer-based policy for imitation learning, especially suited for fine-grained manipulation tasks<br/>- Lightweight (~80M parameters) and data-efficient, typically achieving high success rates with just 50 demonstrations<br/>- Uses an encoder-decoder transformer that processes robot states and camera inputs to generate smooth, chunked action sequences<br/>- Expected training time: ~1.5 hours for 100k steps on NVIDIA A100 GPU with batch size 64[1]<br/><br/>**Diffusion Policy**[3]<br/>- Takes a different approach by starting with Gaussian noise and iteratively removing it to predict actions, rather than using learned embeddings<br/>- Based on the "Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion" paper<br/>- LeRobot provides an easy-to-parse implementation for training<br/><br/>**SmolVLA (Vision-Language-Action Model)**[2][4]<br/>- Compact 450M-parameter open-source VLA model developed by Hugging Face, designed to run on consumer hardware<br/>- Supports asynchronous inference for 30% faster response and 2× task throughput<br/>- Outperforms larger VLAs and ACT on both simulation (LIBERO, Meta-World) and real-world tasks<br/>- Pretrained on open-source community datasets; fine-tuning requires ~50 demonstration episodes<br/>- Expected training time: ~5 hours for 20k steps on NVIDIA A100 GPU with batch size 64[1]<br/><br/>## Framework Features<br/><br/>LeRobot provides ready-to-run Google Colab notebooks for training each policy type, standardized dataset support via the Hugging Face Hub, and optional integration with Weights & Biases for experiment tracking.[1] |

```json
{
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/act",
  "markdown": "# ACT (Action Chunking with Transformers)\n\nACT is a lightweight and efficient policy for imitation learning, especially well-suited for fine-grained manipulation tasks. It's the first model we recommend when you're starting out with LeRobot due to its fast training time, low computational requirements, and strong performance.\n\nWatch this tutorial from the LeRobot team to learn how ACT works: [LeRobot ACT Tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ft73x0LfGpM)\n\n## Model Overview\n\nAction Chunking with Transformers (ACT) was introduced in the paper [Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.13705) by Zhao et al. The policy was designed to enable precise, contact-rich manipulation tasks using affordable hardware and minimal demonstration data.\n\n### Why ACT is Great for Beginners\n\nACT stands out as an excellent starting point for several reasons:\n\n- Data Efficient: Often achieves high success rates with just 50 demonstrations\n- Lightweight: Only ~80M parameters, making it efficient and easy to work with\n- Fast Training: Trains in a few hours on a single GPU\n\n### Architecture\n\nACT uses a transformer-based architecture with three main components:\n\n1. Transformer Decoder: Generates coherent action sequences using cross-attention\n2. Transformer Encoder: Synthesizes information from camera features, joint positions, and a learned latent variable\n3. Vision Backbone: ResNet-18 processes images from multiple camera viewpoints\n\nThe policy takes as input:\n\n- A latent style variable &#96;z&#96; (learned during training, set to zero during inference)\n- Current robot joint positions\n- Multiple RGB images (e.g., from wrist cameras, front/top cameras)\n\nAnd outputs a chunk of &#96;k&#96; future action sequences.\n\n## Installation Requirements\n\n1. ACT is included in the base LeRobot installation, so no additional dependencies are needed!\n2. Install LeRobot by following our [Installation Guide](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/installation).\n\n## Training ACT\n\nACT works seamlessly with the standard LeRobot training pipeline. Here's a complete example for training ACT on your dataset:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nlerobot-train \\\n  --dataset.repo_id=${HF_USER}/your_dataset \\\n  --policy.type=act \\\n  --output_dir=outputs/train/act_your_dataset \\\n  --job_name=act_your_dataset \\\n  --policy.device=cuda \\\n  --wandb.enable=true \\\n  --policy.repo_id=${HF_USER}/act_policy\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Training Tips\n\n1. Batch size: Start with batch size 8 and adjust based on your GPU memory\n2. Training duration: Expect a few hours for 100k training steps on a single GPU\n3. Start with defaults: ACT's default hyperparameters work well for most tasks\n\n### Train using Google Colab\n\nIf your local computer doesn't have a powerful GPU, you can utilize Google Colab to train your model by following the [ACT training notebook](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/notebooks#training-act).\n\n## Evaluating ACT\n\nOnce training is complete, you can evaluate your ACT policy using the &#96;lerobot-record&#96; command with your trained policy. This will run inference and record evaluation episodes:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nlerobot-record \\\n  --robot.type=so100_follower \\\n  --robot.port=/dev/ttyACM0 \\\n  --robot.id=my_robot \\\n  --robot.cameras=\"{ front: {type: opencv, index_or_path: 0, width: 640, height: 480, fps: 30}}\" \\\n  --display_data=true \\\n  --dataset.repo_id=${HF_USER}/eval_act_your_dataset \\\n  --dataset.num_episodes=10 \\\n  --dataset.single_task=\"Your task description\" \\\n  --dataset.streaming_encoding=true \\\n  --dataset.encoder_threads=2 \\\n  # --dataset.vcodec=auto \\\n  --policy.path=${HF_USER}/act_policy\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;"
}
```

{
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/smolvla",
  "markdown": "# SmolVLA\n\nSmolVLA is Hugging Face’s lightweight foundation model for robotics. Designed for easy fine-tuning on LeRobot datasets, it helps accelerate your development!\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nFigure 1. SmolVLA takes as input (i) multiple cameras views, (ii) the\nrobot’s current sensorimotor state, and (iii) a natural language\ninstruction, encoded into contextual features used to condition the action\nexpert when generating an action chunk.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Set Up Your Environment\n\n1. Install SmolVLA dependencies by running: pip install -e \".[smolvla]\"\n\n2. Install LeRobot by following our [Installation Guide](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/installation).\n\n## Collect a dataset\n\nSmolVLA is a base model, so fine-tuning on your own data is required for optimal performance in your setup.\nWe recommend recording ~50 episodes of your task as a starting point. Follow our guide to get started: [Recording a Dataset](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/il_robots)\n\nIn your dataset, make sure to have enough demonstrations per each variation (e.g. the cube position on the table if it is cube pick-place task) you are introducing.\n\nWe recommend checking out the dataset linked below for reference that was used in the [SmolVLA paper](https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.01844):\n\n🔗 [SVLA SO100 PickPlace](https://huggingface.co/spaces/lerobot/visualize_dataset?path=%2Flerobot%2Fsvla_so100_pickplace%2Fepisode_0)\n\nIn this dataset, we recorded 50 episodes across 5 distinct cube positions. For each position, we collected 10 episodes of pick-and-place interactions. This structure, repeating each variation several times, helped the model generalize better. We tried similar dataset with 25 episodes, and it was not enough leading to a bad performance. So, the data quality and quantity is definitely a key.\nAfter you have your dataset available on the Hub, you are good to go to use our finetuning script to adapt SmolVLA to your application.\n\n## Finetune SmolVLA on your data\n\nUse [&#96;smolvla_base&#96;](https://hf.co/lerobot/smolvla_base), our pretrained 450M model, and fine-tune it on your data.\nTraining the model for 20k steps will roughly take ~4 hrs on a single A100 GPU. You should tune the number of steps based on performance and your use-case.\n\nIf you don't have a gpu device, you can train using our notebook on [](https://colab.research.google.com/github/huggingface/notebooks/blob/main/lerobot/training-smolvla.ipynb)\n\nPass your dataset to the training script using &#96;--dataset.repo_id&#96;. If you want to test your installation, run the following command where we use one of the datasets we collected for the [SmolVLA Paper](https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.01844).\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\ncd lerobot &amp;&amp; lerobot-train \\\n  --policy.path=lerobot/smolvla_base \\\n  --dataset.repo_id=${HF_USER}/mydataset \\\n  --batch_size=64 \\\n  --steps=20000 \\\n  --output_dir=outputs/train/my_smolvla \\\n  --job_name=my_smolvla_training \\\n  --policy.device=cuda \\\n  --wandb.enable=true\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou can start with a small batch size and increase it incrementally, if the\nGPU allows it, as long as loading times remain short.\n\nFine-tuning is an art. For a complete overview of the options for finetuning, run\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nlerobot-train --help\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nFigure 2: Comparison of SmolVLA across task variations. From left to right:\n(1) pick-place cube counting, (2) pick-place cube counting, (3) pick-place\ncube counting under perturbations, and (4) generalization on pick-and-place\nof the lego block with real-world SO101.\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Evaluate the finetuned model and run it in real-time\n\nSimilarly for when recording an episode, it is recommended that you are logged in to the HuggingFace Hub. You can follow the corresponding steps: [Record a dataset](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/il_robots).\nOnce you are logged in, you can run inference in your setup by doing:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;bash\nlerobot-record \\\n  --robot.type=so101_follower \\\n  --robot.port=/dev/ttyACM0 \\ # &lt;- Use your port\n  --robot.id=my_blue_follower_arm \\ # &lt;- Use your robot id\n  --robot.cameras=\"{ front: {type: opencv, index_or_path: 8, width: 640, height: 480, fps: 30}}\" \\ # &lt;- Use your cameras\n  --dataset.single_task=\"Grasp a lego block and put it in the bin.\" \\ # &lt;- Use the same task description you used in your dataset recording\n  --dataset.repo_id=${HF_USER}/eval_DATASET_NAME_test \\  # &lt;- This will be the dataset name on HF Hub\n  --dataset.episode_time_s=50 \\\n  --dataset.num_episodes=10 \\\n  --dataset.streaming_encoding=true \\\n  --dataset.encoder_threads=2 \\\n  # --dataset.vcodec=auto \\\n  # &lt;- Teleop optional if you want to teleoperate in between episodes \\\n  # --teleop.type=so100_leader \\\n  # --teleop.port=/dev/ttyACM0 \\\n  # --teleop.id=my_red_leader_arm \\\n  --policy.path=HF_USER/FINETUNE_MODEL_NAME # &lt;- Use your fine-tuned model\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nDepending on your evaluation setup, you can configure the duration and the number of episodes to record for your evaluation suite."
}

[tiiuae/lerobot-edge](https://github.com/tiiuae/lerobot-edge)<br/><br/># LeRobot: An Open-Source Library for End-to-End Robot Learning<br/><br/>**Paper ID:** arXiv 2602.22818<br/><br/>## Abstract Summary<br/><br/>LeRobot is an open-source library designed to integrate across the entire robot learning stack, addressing fragmentation in the field caused by closed-source, component-specific tools.[1] The library encompasses everything from low-level middleware communication for motor controls to large-scale dataset collection, storage, and streaming.[1]<br/><br/>## Key Features<br/><br/>- **Real-world focus:** Designed with strong emphasis on real-world robotics, supporting accessible hardware platforms while remaining extensible to new embodiments[1]<br/>- **State-of-the-art algorithms:** Implements efficient versions of various robot learning algorithms from multiple prominent paradigms, plus a generalized asynchronous inference stack[1]<br/>- **Scalable learning:** Emphasizes scalable learning approaches that improve directly with more data and compute, rather than relying on hand-crafted techniques[1]<br/>- **Accessibility and reproducibility:** Lowers barriers to entry for researchers and practitioners while providing a platform for reproducible, state-of-the-art robot learning[1]<br/><br/>## Context<br/><br/>The library was created in response to recent progress in robot learning accelerated by affordable teleoperation systems, large-scale openly available datasets, and scalable learning methods.[1] It is authored by a team led by Remi Cadene and others, with support from Hugging Face.[1] |

[InterACT: Inter-dependency Aware Action Chunking with Hierarchical Attention Transformers for Bimanual Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/html/2409.07914v2)<br/>3. [Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware \| OpenReview](https://openreview.net/forum?id=e8Eu1lqLaf)<br/>4. [Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware - ADS](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2023arXiv230413705Z/abstract)<br/>5. [Action Chunking with Transformers - ACT — Intel Embodied Intelligence SDK Intel Embodied Intelligence SDK documentation](https://eci.intel.com/embodied-sdk-docs/content/developer_tools_tutorials/model_tutorials/model_act.html)<br/><br/># Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT) Paper Summary<br/><br/>The paper "Learning Fine-Grained Bimanual Manipulation with Low-Cost Hardware" (arXiv:2304.13705) was authored by Tony Z. Zhao, Vikash Kumar, Sergey Levine, and Chelsea Finn, submitted in April 2023.[1]<br/><br/>## Key Contribution<br/><br/>The paper introduces **Action Chunking with Transformers (ACT)**, a novel imitation learning algorithm designed to enable low-cost robotic systems to perform precise fine-grained bimanual manipulation tasks.[1][3]<br/><br/>## Problem Addressed<br/><br/>Fine manipulation tasks like threading cable ties or slotting a battery are difficult for robots because they require precision, careful coordination of contact forces, and closed-loop visual feedback. Traditional approaches require expensive high-end robots and careful calibration.[1][3]<br/><br/>## Solution<br/><br/>ACT learns a generative model over action sequences rather than predicting single actions, which reduces the effective horizon and mitigates compounding policy errors that plague traditional imitation learning in high-precision domains.[1][3] The system performs end-to-end imitation learning from real demonstrations collected via custom teleoperation.[1]<br/><br/>## Results<br/><br/>ACT enabled robots to learn 6 difficult real-world tasks—such as opening a translucent condiment cup and slotting a battery—with 80-90% success rates using only 10 minutes of demonstration data.[1][3]<br/><br/>## Architecture<br/><br/>The model uses an observation encoder (processing images and sensor data), a transformer network to model temporal dependencies between action chunks, and an action decoder to convert predictions into control commands.[5] |

</details>
</tool-use>

[Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/html/2303.04137v5)<br/>2. [Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion \| Toyota Research Institute](https://www.tri.global/research/diffusion-policy-visuomotor-policy-learning-action-diffusion)<br/>3. [Diffusion policy: Visuomotor policy learning via action diffusion](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/02783649241273668)<br/>4. [Diffusion Policy](https://diffusion-policy.cs.columbia.edu/)<br/>5. [Paper page - Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion](https://huggingface.co/papers/2303.04137)<br/><br/># Diffusion Policy: Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion<br/><br/>Diffusion Policy is a novel approach to robot behavior generation that represents a robot's visuomotor policy as a conditional denoising diffusion process.[1] The method was introduced by researchers from Columbia University, Toyota Research Institute, and MIT.<br/><br/>## Key Advantages<br/><br/>The approach offers several significant benefits for robot learning:[1]<br/>- Gracefully handles multimodal action distributions<br/>- Suitable for high-dimensional action spaces<br/>- Exhibits impressive training stability<br/><br/>## Technical Approach<br/><br/>Diffusion Policy learns the gradient of the action-distribution score function and iteratively optimizes with respect to this gradient field during inference through stochastic Langevin dynamics steps.[1] Key technical contributions include incorporation of receding horizon control, visual conditioning, and a time-series diffusion transformer.[1]<br/><br/>## Performance<br/><br/>The method was benchmarked across 12-15 tasks from 4 different robot manipulation benchmarks (including Robomimic, IBC, BET, and Relay Policy Learning environments).[1][4] Diffusion Policy consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art robot learning methods with an average improvement of 46.9%.[1]<br/><br/>## Publication<br/><br/>The work was published at Robotics: Science and Systems (RSS) 2023 and later in The International Journal of Robotics Research (IJRR) 2024.[4] Code, data, and training details are publicly available at diffusion-policy.cs.columbia.edu.[1] |

</details>
</tool-use>

[SmolVLA: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Model trained on Lerobot Community Data](https://huggingface.co/blog/smolvla)<br/>2. [SmolVLA](https://huggingface.co/docs/lerobot/en/smolvla)<br/>3. [lerobot/smolvla_base · Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/lerobot/smolvla_base)<br/>4. [SmolVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model for Modern Robotics](https://smolvla.net/index_en.html)<br/><br/># SmolVLA: Hugging Face's Lightweight Robotics Foundation Model<br/><br/>SmolVLA is a compact 450M-parameter Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model developed by Hugging Face for robotics.[1] It represents a significant shift toward democratizing robotics AI by offering an open-source, efficient alternative to proprietary models.<br/><br/>## Key Features<br/><br/>SmolVLA supports asynchronous inference for 30% faster response times and 2× task throughput.[1] Despite its small size, it outperforms much larger VLAs and baselines like ACT on both simulation tasks (LIBERO, Meta-World) and real-world applications (SO100, SO101).[1] The model achieves approximately 78.3% average success rate in real-world tasks.[5]<br/><br/>## Training & Community Data<br/><br/>The model is pretrained exclusively on open-source, community-shared datasets under the LeRobot tag with compatible licenses.[1] This community-driven approach allows researchers to access high-quality robotics data without proprietary restrictions. SmolVLA accepts multiple camera views, the robot's sensorimotor state, and natural language instructions as input to generate robot actions.[2]<br/><br/>## Accessibility & Deployment<br/><br/>SmolVLA can be trained on consumer-grade hardware, including a single GPU, making it accessible to broader research communities.[1][3] Fine-tuning on custom datasets requires approximately ~50 episodes and roughly 4 hours on a single A100 GPU for 20,000 training steps.[2]<br/><br/>The model addresses a critical gap in robotics by providing an efficient, reproducible foundation that doesn't require costly hardware setups or extensive engineering resources.[1] |

</details>
</tool-use>

{
  "url": "https://huggingface.co/blog/smolvla",
  "markdown": "SmolVLA: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Model trained on Lerobot Community Data\n\n[Back to Articles](https://huggingface.co/blog)\n\n# SmolVLA: Efficient Vision-Language-Action Model trained on Lerobot Community Data\n\nPublished June 3, 2025\n\n[Update on GitHub](https://github.com/huggingface/blog/blob/main/smolvla.md)\n\n[Upvote 330](https://huggingface.co/login?next=%2Fblog%2Fsmolvla)\n\n- +324\n\n[Dana Aubakirova danaaubakirova Follow](https://huggingface.co/danaaubakirova)\n\n[Andres Marafioti andito Follow](https://huggingface.co/andito)\n\n[merve merve Follow](https://huggingface.co/merve)\n\n[Aritra Roy Gosthipaty ariG23498 Follow](https://huggingface.co/ariG23498)\n\n[Francesco Capuano fracapuano Follow](https://huggingface.co/fracapuano)\n\n[Loubna Ben Allal loubnabnl Follow](https://huggingface.co/loubnabnl)\n\n[Pedro Cuenca pcuenq Follow](https://huggingface.co/pcuenq)\n\n[Mustafa Shukor mshukor Follow](https://huggingface.co/mshukor)\n\n[Remi Cadene cadene Follow](https://huggingface.co/cadene)\n\n## 🧭TL;DR\n\nToday, we introduce [SmolVLA](https://huggingface.co/lerobot/smolvla_base), a compact (450M), open-source Vision-Language-Action model for robotics that runs on consumer hardware.\n\n- Supports asynchronous inference for 30% faster response and 2× task throughput.\n- SmolVLA-450M outperforms much larger VLAs and strong baselines such as [ACT](https://huggingface.co/papers/2401.02117) on simulation (LIBERO, Meta-World) and real-world tasks ([SO100, SO101](https://github.com/TheRobotStudio/SO-ARM100)).\n- Pretrained only on compatibly licensed, open-source community-shared datasets under the [lerobot](https://huggingface.co/datasets?other=lerobot&amp;amp;sort=trending) tag.\n\nUseful links:\n\n- Paper: [https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.01844](https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.01844)\n- Base model [https://huggingface.co/lerobot/smolvla_base](https://huggingface.co/lerobot/smolvla_base)\n- Hardware used to train and evaluate SO-100/101: [https://github.com/TheRobotStudio/SO-ARM100](https://github.com/TheRobotStudio/SO-ARM100)\n\n## 📚 Table of Contents\n\n🚀 How to Use SmolVLA?\n\n- Train from Scratch\n- Finetune the Pretrained Model\n- Install\n\n🧠 Method\n\nMain Architecture\n\n- Action Expert: Flow Matching Transformer\n- Vision-Language Model (VLM)\n\nDesign Choices for Efficiency and Robustness\n\n- Interleaved Cross and Self-Attention\n- Faster Inference via Layer Skipping\n- Visual Token Reduction\n\n📦 Community Datasets\n\n- Standardizing Camera Views\n- Improving Task Annotations\n\n## Introduction\n\nOver the past few years, Transformers have driven remarkable progress in AI, from language models capable of human-like reasoning to multimodal systems that understand both images and text. However, in real-world robotics, advancements have been much slower. Robots still struggle to generalize across diverse objects, environments, and tasks. This limited progress stems from a lack of high-quality, diverse data and the absence of models that can reason and act like humans in the physical world.\n\nIn response to these challenges, the field has recently turned to vision-language-action (VLA) models, which aim to unify perception, language understanding, and action prediction within a single architecture. VLAs typically take as input raw visual observations and natural language instructions, and output corresponding robot actions. While promising, much of the recent progress in VLAs remains locked behind proprietary models trained on large-scale private datasets, often requiring costly hardware setups and extensive engineering resources. As a result, the broader robotics research community faces significant barriers in reproducing and building upon these models.\n\nSmolVLA addresses this gap by offering an open-source, compact, and efficient VLA model that can be trained on consumer-grade hardware using only publicly available datasets. By releasing not only model weights but also using very affordable open-source hardware, SmolVLA aims to democratize access to vision-language-action models and accelerate research toward generalist robotic agents.\n\nFigure 1: Comparison of SmolVLA across task variations. From left to right: (1) asynchronous pick-place cube counting, (2) synchronous pick-place cube counting, (3) pick-place cube counting under perturbations, and (4) generalization on pick-and-place of the lego block with real-world SO101.\n\n## Meet SmolVLA!\n\nSmolVLA-450M is our open-source, compact yet capable VLA model. It is:\n\n- Can be tested and deployed on very affordable hardware (SO-100, SO-101, LeKiwi, etc.)\n- Released with full training and inference recipes\n- Trained on public, community-shared robotics data\n- Small enough to run on CPU, train on a single consumer GPU, or even a MacBook!\n- 📣 Call to Action\n\nInspired by the training paradigms of Large Language Models (LLMs), SmolVLA goes through a pretraining phase on general manipulation data, followed by task-specific post-training. Architecturally, it combines Transformers with flow-matching decoders, and is optimized for speed and low-latency inference with the following design choices:\n\n- Leveraging smaller pretrained VLMs\n- Using fewer visual tokens\n- Interleaving self-attention and cross-attention blocks\n- Skipping half of the layers of the vision model for faster inference and smaller size\n\nDespite using fewer than 30k training episodes—an order of magnitude less than other VLAs—SmolVLA matches or exceeds the performance of much larger models, both in simulation and the real world.\n\nTo make real-time robotics easier to use, we introduce an asynchronous inference stack. This technology separates how robots perform actions from how they understand what they see and hear. Because of this separation, robots can respond more quickly in fast-changing environments.\n\nFigure 2. SmolVLA takes as input a sequence of RGB images from multiple cameras, the robot’s current sensorimotor state, and a natural language instruction. The VLM encodes these into contextual features, which condition the action expert to generate a continuous sequence of actions.\n\n## 🚀 How to Use SmolVLA?\n\nSmolVLA is designed to be easy to use and integrate—whether you're finetuning on your own data or plugging it into an existing robotics stack.\n\n### Install\n\nFirst, install the required dependencies:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\ngit clone https://github.com/huggingface/lerobot.git\ncd lerobot\npip install -e \".[smolvla]\"\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Finetune the pretrained model\n\nUse [smolvla_base](https://hf.co/lerobot/smolvla_base), our pretrained 450M model, with the lerobot training framework:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\npython lerobot/scripts/train.py \\\n  --policy.path=lerobot/smolvla_base \\\n  --dataset.repo_id=lerobot/svla_so100_stacking \\\n  --batch_size=64 \\\n  --steps=20000  # 10% of training budget\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Train from scratch\n\n​​If you'd like to build from the architecture (pretrained VLM + action expert) rather than a pretrained checkpoint:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\npython lerobot/scripts/train.py \\\n  --policy.type=smolvla \\\n  --dataset.repo_id=lerobot/svla_so100_stacking \\\n  --batch_size=64 \\\n  --steps=200000\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\nYou can also load&#96;SmolVLAPolicy&#96; directly:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nfrom lerobot.common.policies.smolvla.modeling_smolvla import SmolVLAPolicy\npolicy = SmolVLAPolicy.from_pretrained(\"lerobot/smolvla_base\")\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n## Method\n\nSmolVLA is not only a lightweight yet capable model, but also a method for training and evaluating generalist robotics policies. In this section, we introduce the model architecture behind SmolVLA and the asynchronous inference setup used for evaluation, which has proven to be more adaptable and capable of faster recovery.\n\nSmolVLA consists of two core components: a Vision-Language Model (VLM) that processes multimodal inputs and an action expert that outputs robot control commands. Below, we share the details of the main components of SmolVLA architecture and the Asynchronous Inference. More details can be found in our [technical report](https://huggingface.co/papers/2506.01844).\n\n### Main Architecture\n\n#### Vision-Language Model (VLM)\n\nWe use [SmolVLM2](https://huggingface.co/HuggingFaceTB/SmolVLM2-500M-Video-Instruct) as our VLM backbone. It’s optimized for multi-image inputs and consists of a SigLIP vision encoder and a [SmolLM2](https://huggingface.co/HuggingFaceTB/SmolLM2-1.7B-Instruct) language decoder.\n\n- Sensorimotor states are projected into a single token using a linear layer to align with the token dimension of the language model.\n- Language instructions are tokenized and fed directly into the decoder.\n- Image tokens are extracted via the vision encoder\n\nThe decoder layers process concatenated image, language, and state tokens. The resulting features are then passed to the action expert.\n\n#### Action Expert: Flow Matching Transformer\n\nSmolVLA’s action expert is a compact transformer (~100M parameters) that generates action chunks, i.e. sequences of future robot actions, conditioned on the VLM’s outputs. It is trained using a flow matching objective, which teaches the model to guide noisy samples back to the ground truth. In contrast, while discrete action representations (e.g., via tokenization) are powerful, they often require autoregressive decoding, which is slow and inefficient at inference time. While flow matching allows direct, non-autoregressive prediction of continuous actions, enabling real-time control with high precision.\n\nMore intuitively, during training, we add random noise to the robot’s real action sequences and ask the model to predict the “correction vector” that brings them back to the correct trajectory. This forms a smooth vector field over the action space, helping the model learn accurate and stable control policies.\n\nWe implement this using a transformer architecture with interleaved attention blocks (see the figure 2), and reduce its hidden size to 75% of the VLM’s, keeping the model lightweight for deployment.\n\n### Design Choices for Efficiency and Robustness\n\nWhile combining a vision-language model with an action prediction module is a common design pattern in recent VLA systems—such as Pi0, GR00T, Diffusion Policy — we identified several architectural choices that significantly enhance the robustness and performance. In SmolVLA, we apply three key techniques: reducing the number of visual tokens, skipping upper layers in the VLM, and interleaving cross- and self-attention layers in the action expert.\n\n#### Visual Token Reduction\n\nHigh-resolution images improve perception but can significantly slow down inference. To strike a balance, SmolVLA limits the number of visual tokens to 64 per frame during both training and inference. For example, a 512×512 image is compressed into just 64 tokens, instead of 1024, using PixelShuffle as an efficient shuffling technique. While the underlying Vision-Language Model (VLM) was originally pretrained using image tiling for broader coverage, SmolVLA uses only the global image at runtime to keep inference lightweight and fast.\n\n#### Faster Inference via Layer Skipping\n\nRather than always relying on the final layer of the VLM—which can be expensive and sometimes suboptimal—we use features from intermediate layers. Prior work has shown that early layers often provide better representations for downstream tasks. In SmolVLA, the action expert only attends to VLM features up to a configurable layer NN during training, set to half the total layers. This halves the compute cost of both the VLM and the action expert, significantly speeding up inference with minimal performance loss.\n\n#### Interleaved Cross and Self-Attention\n\nInside the action expert, attention layers alternate between:\n\n- Self-attention (SA), where action tokens attend to each other (causally—only to the past)\n- Cross-attention (CA), where action tokens attend to the VLM’s features\n\nWe found that this interleaved design is both lighter and more effective than using full attention blocks. Models that rely only on CA or only on SA tend to sacrifice either smoothness or grounding.\n\nIn SmolVLA, CA ensures that actions are well-conditioned on perception and instructions, while SA improves temporal smoothness—especially critical for real-world control, where jittery predictions can result in unsafe or unstable behavior.\n\n## Asynchronous Inference\n\nFigure 3. Asynchronous inference. Illustration of the asynchronous inference stack. Note that the policy can be run on a remote server, possibly with GPUs.\n\nModern visuomotor policies output action chunks—sequences of actions to execute. There are two ways to manage them:\n\n- Asynchronous (async): While executing the current chunk, the robot already sends the latest observation to a Policy Server (possibly hosted on GPU) for the next chunk. This avoids idle time and improves reactivity.\n- Synchronous (sync): The robot executes a chunk, then pauses while the next one is computed. Simple, but causes a delay where the robot can't react to new inputs.\n\nOur async stack decouples action execution from chunk prediction, resulting in higher adaptability, and the complete lack of execution lags at runtime. It relies on the following key mechanisms:\n\n- 3. Chunk fusion: Overlapping actions from successive chunks are stitched with a simple merge rule to avoid jitter.\n- 2. Decoupled threads: Control loop keeps executing → inference happens in parallel (non-blocking).\n- 1. Early trigger: When the queue length falls below a threshold (e.g., 70%), we send an observation to a Policy Server, calling for a new action chunk.\n\nWe are really excited about releasing asynchronous inference because it guarantees greater adaptability and improved performance without changing the model. In short, async inference keeps the robot responsive by overlapping execution and remote prediction.\n\n## Community Datasets\n\nWhile vision and language models thrive on web-scale datasets like LAION, ImageNet, and Common Crawl, robotics lacks a comparable resource. There’s no “Internet of robots.” Instead, data is fragmented across robot types, sensors, control schemes, and formats—forming disconnected \"data islands\". In our [previous post](https://huggingface.co/blog/lerobot-datasets), we explored how this fragmentation could be resolved through open, collaborative efforts. Just as ImageNet catalyzed breakthroughs in computer vision by providing a large, diverse benchmark, we believe that community-driven robotics datasets can play the same foundational role for generalist robot policies.\n\nSmolVLA is our first step toward that vision: It is pretrained on a curated mix of publicly available, community-contributed datasets designed to reflect real-world variation. Rather than optimizing for dataset size alone, we focus on diversity: a range of behaviors, camera viewpoints, and embodiments that promote transfer and generalization.\n\nAll training data used in SmolVLA comes from LeRobot Community Datasets , robotics datasets shared on the Hugging Face Hub under the&#96;lerobot&#96; tag. Collected in diverse settings, from labs to living rooms, these datasets represent an open, decentralized effort to scale real-world robot data.\n\nFigure 4. A glimpse of the community dataset. Special thanks to Ville Kuosmanen for creating the visualization. Unlike academic benchmarks, community datasets naturally capture messy, realistic interactions: varied lighting, suboptimal demonstrations, unconventional objects, and heterogeneous control schemes. This kind of diversity will be very useful for learning robust, general-purpose representations.\n\nWe used a custom [filtering tool](https://huggingface.co/spaces/Beegbrain/FilterLeRobotData) created by [Alexandre Chapin](https://huggingface.co/Beegbrain) and [Ville Kuosmanen](https://huggingface.co/villekuosmanen) to select datasets based on frame count, visual quality, and task coverage. After a meticulous manual review (special thanks to Marina Barannikov), we curated a collection of 487 high-quality datasets focused on the SO100 robotic arm, standardized at 30 FPS. This yielded around 10 million frames—at least one order of magnitude smaller than other popular benchmark datasets, yet significantly more diverse.\n\n### Improving Task Annotations\n\nA common issue across community datasets was noisy or missing task descriptions. Many episodes lacked annotations or included vague labels like “task desc” or “Move”, “Pick”. To improve quality and standardize the textual input across datasets, we used [Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct](https://huggingface.co/Qwen/Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) to generate concise, action-oriented descriptions.\n\nGiven sample frames and the original label, the model was prompted to rewrite the instruction in under 30 characters, starting with an action verb (e.g., “Pick,” “Place,” “Open”).\n\nThe prompt used is:\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\nHere is a current task description: {current_task}. Generate a very short, clear, and complete one-sentence describing the action performed by the robot arm (max 30 characters). Do not include unnecessary words.\nBe concise.\nHere are some examples: Pick up the cube and place it in the box, open the drawer and so on.\nStart directly with an action verb like “Pick”, “Place”, “Open”, etc.\nSimilar to the provided examples, what is the main action done by the robot arm?\n\n&#96;&#96;&#96;\n\n### Standardizing Camera Views\n\nAnother challenge was inconsistent camera naming. Some datasets used clear names like top or&#96;wrist.right&#96;, while others used ambiguous labels like&#96;images.laptop&#96;, which varied in meaning. To fix this, we manually went through the datasets and mapped each camera view to a standardized scheme:&#96;OBS_IMAGE_1&#96;: Top-down view&#96;OBS_IMAGE_2&#96;: Wrist-mounted view&#96;OBS_IMAGE_3+&#96;: Additional viewpoints\n\nWe further isolate the contributions of community dataset pretraining and multitask finetuning. Without pretraining on the LeRobot community datasets, SmolVLA initially achieves 51.7% success on SO100. After pretraining on community-collected data, performance jumps to 78.3%, a +26.6% absolute improvement. Multitask finetuning further boosts performance, showing strong task transfer capabilities even in low-data regimes.\n\nTable 1. Impact of Pretraining on Community Datasets and Multitask Finetuning.\n\n## Results\n\nWe evaluate SmolVLA across simulation and real-world benchmarks to test its generalization, efficiency, and robustness. Despite being compact, It consistently outperforms or matches the performance of significantly larger models and policies pretrained on higher-scale robotics data.\n\nTable 2. SmolVLA Performance on Simulation Benchmarks.\n\nTable 3. SmolVLA vs Baselines on Real-World Tasks (SO100).\n\nIn real-world settings, SmolVLA is evaluated on two diverse suites: SO100 and SO101. These tasks include pick-place, stacking, and sorting, with both in-distribution and out-of-distribution object configurations. On SO101, SmolVLA also excels in generalization:\n\nTable 4. Generalization of SmolVLA to New Embodiment (SO101) vs ACT..\n\nFinally, we evaluate SmolVLA under synchronous and asynchronous inference modes. Async inference decouples action execution from model inference, allowing the policy to react while the robot is moving.\n\nBoth modes achieve similar task success (≈78%), but async inference:\n\n- Enables 2× more completions in fixed-time settings (19 vs. 9 cubes)\n- Completes tasks ~30% faster (9.7s vs. 13.75s)\n\nThis results in more responsive and robust real-world performance, especially in dynamic environments with shifting objects or external disturbances.\n\nFigure 5. Asynchronous vs. Synchronous Inference in Real-World Tasks. (a) Task success rates (%), (b) average completion time(s), and (c) number of tasks completed within a fixed time window.\n\n## Conclusion\n\nSmolVLA is our contribution to building robotics foundation models that are open, efficient, and reproducible. Despite its small size, it matches or outperforms larger, proprietary models across a range of real-world and simulated tasks. By relying solely on community-contributed datasets and affordable hardware, SmolVLA lowers the barrier to entry for researchers, educators, and hobbyists alike. But this is just the beginning. SmolVLA is more than just a model — it's part of a growing open-source movement toward scalable, collaborative robotics.\n\n## Call to Action:\n\n- 📫 Stay in touch: Follow the [LeRobot organization](https://huggingface.co/lerobot) and [Discord server](https://discord.com/invite/ttk5CV6tUw) for updates, tutorials, and new releases.\n- 🌍 Spread the word. Share SmolVLA with fellow researchers, developers, or educators interested in efficient, real-time robotic policies.\n- 📊 Contribute. Improve datasets, report issues, suggest new ideas. Every contribution helps.\n- 💬 Join the blog discussion. Drop your questions, ideas, or feedback in the discussion below. We’re happy to help with integration, training, or deployment.\n- 🤖 Upload the dataset! Got a robot? Collect and share your data using the lerobot format. Help expand the community dataset that powers SmolVLA.\n- 🔧 Try it out! Abstract:Robotics is undergoing a significant transformation powered by advances in high-level control techniques based on machine learning, giving rise to the field of robot learning. Recent progress in robot learning has been accelerated by the increasing availability of affordable teleoperation systems, large-scale openly available datasets, and scalable learning-based methods. However, development in the field of robot learning is often slowed by fragmented, closed-source tools designed to only address specific sub-components within the robotics stack. In this paper, we present \\texttt{lerobot}, an open-source library that integrates across the entire robot learning stack, from low-level middleware communication for motor controls to large-scale dataset collection, storage and streaming. The library is designed with a strong focus on real-world robotics, supporting accessible hardware platforms while remaining extensible to new embodiments. It also supports efficient implementations for various state-of-the-art robot learning algorithms from multiple prominent paradigms, as well as a generalized asynchronous inference stack. Unlike traditional pipelines which heavily rely on hand-crafted techniques, \\texttt{lerobot} emphasizes scalable learning approaches that improve directly with more data and compute. Designed for accessibility, scalability, and openness, \\texttt{lerobot} lowers the barrier to entry for researchers and practitioners to robotics while providing a platform for reproducible, `LeRobot` 可以把它理解成一个“面向真实机器人学习的统一框架”：它不是单独的模型，也不是单独的数据集工具，而是把 `机器人接入`、`示教采集`、`标准数据集`、`策略训练`、`仿真评估`、`真实部署`、`Hub 分享` 串成一条完整工作流。官方首页、README 和论文的表述都非常一致: 它的核心目标是降低机器人学习门槛，用开放数据和预训练模型推动真实世界机器人学习。

从仓库 `README.md` 看，官方对 LeRobot 的自我定位很清楚：

```17:25:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/README.md
**LeRobot** aims to provide models, datasets, and tools for real-world robotics in PyTorch. The goal is to lower the barrier to entry so that everyone can contribute to and benefit from shared datasets and pretrained models.

🤗 A hardware-agnostic, Python-native interface that standardizes control across diverse platforms, from low-cost arms (SO-100) to humanoids.

🤗 A standardized, scalable LeRobotDataset format (Parquet + MP4 or images) hosted on the Hugging Face Hub, enabling efficient storage, streaming and visualization of massive robotic datasets.

🤗 State-of-the-art policies that have been shown to transfer to the real-world ready for training and deployment.
```

## 一、先建立一套知识体系
我建议把 LeRobot 理解成 6 层。

1. `硬件接入层`
LeRobot 提供统一的 `Robot`、`Teleoperator`、`Camera`、`Motor` 抽象，把不同硬件包装成相似接口。它支持从低成本机械臂到更复杂 embodiment，也允许你自己实现接口接入自定义机器人。它适合的场景包括：低成本教学平台、实验室机械臂、双臂示教、手机/手柄/键盘遥操作、以及把已有机器人快速接进学习框架。

2. `数据层`
LeRobot 把数据集当成第一公民，核心是 `LeRobotDataset`。它不是简单的 `npz` 或一堆图片，而是把状态/动作放在 `Parquet`，视觉放在 `MP4` 或图片，元信息放在 `json/parquet`，并直接围绕 Hugging Face Hub 组织下载、上传、流式读取、可视化、编辑。这一点非常关键，因为机器人学习里最难的往往不是“写个模型”，而是“把数据组织成可复用资产”。

3. `处理契约层`
LeRobot 的一个很强但容易被忽视的设计是 `processor pipeline`。它把 observation/action 的重命名、归一化、环境适配、设备适配做成独立流水线，而不是揉进模型内部。这样同一个策略可以在不同数据集、不同环境、不同真实机器人之间迁移，只改 processor 或 config 即可。

4. `策略/模型层`
LeRobot 不是只支持一个算法，而是提供统一训练/部署接口承载多类策略。当前你最该关注的是三条路线：
- `ACT`：轻量、数据效率高，适合精细操作和入门。
- `Diffusion Policy`：更擅长多峰动作分布和复杂时序控制，但推理和训练更重。
- `SmolVLA`：把视觉、语言、动作统一起来，是 LeRobot 向 foundation model / VLA 方向迈进的代表。
另外仓库和文档还覆盖 `VQ-BeT`、`Pi0/Pi0.5`、`GR00T`、`HIL-SERL` 等，说明它已经不只是 imitation learning 教程库，而是在扩成更广泛的机器人学习平台。

5. `运行工作流层`
LeRobot 真正的价值不在“有模型”，而在“有一条统一工作流”：
- `lerobot-record`：采集示教数据，也能挂策略做真实机器人评测。
- `lerobot-replay`：把数据集里的动作轨迹重新回放到机器人。
- `lerobot-train`：直接从标准数据集训练策略。
- `lerobot-eval`：在仿真 benchmark 上批量 rollout 评估。
- `async inference`：把推理和控制解耦，支持远程 GPU 推理、机器人本地执行。
这使它非常适合研究迭代: 录数据 -> 训模型 -> 评估 -> 再录数据。

6. `生态协作层`
LeRobot 深度绑定 Hugging Face Hub。模型、数据集、配置、processor、可视化和教程都围绕 Hub 展开。它的思想不是“每个实验各写一套私有脚本”，而是“把机器人学习里的 artifact 标准化并共享”。

## 二、LeRobot 有什么功能，适合什么场景
从官方文档、README、示例和源码综合看，LeRobot 主要能做这些事。

1. `真实机器人示教采集`
你可以接入机械臂、相机和遥操作设备，录制带图像、状态、动作、任务描述的数据集。这是它最核心的能力之一，特别适合 imitation learning。

2. `标准化数据集管理`
它能创建、加载、回放、编辑、拆分、合并、可视化数据集，并上传到 Hub。对于团队协作和论文复现非常有价值。

3. `策略训练与微调`
可以直接用 `lerobot-train` 训练 `ACT`、`Diffusion Policy`、`SmolVLA` 等模型，或者从 Hub 上已有 checkpoint 继续 finetune。

4. `仿真评估与 benchmark`
支持在 `PushT`、`LIBERO`、`MetaWorld` 等环境里评估策略。这让它不只用于真实机器人，还能做算法对比和离线开发。

5. `真实机器人部署`
你可以把训练好的策略挂到真实机器人上执行任务，尤其适合低成本机械臂做抓取、放置、开盖、插装、桌面操作等任务。

6. `异步推理`
这是 LeRobot 很有特色的一点。对于 action chunking 或 VLA 模型，推理较重，LeRobot 可以让机器人一边执行当前动作块，一边把新观测发到远端策略服务器请求下一块动作，提高响应性。

7. `教育与开源协作`
因为它支持低成本硬件、Hub 数据共享、Colab/脚本化训练，所以很适合教学、hackathon、实验室新人成长和社区复现。

它特别适合的应用场景有：
- `低成本桌面机械臂学习`
- `人类示教驱动的抓取/放置/装配`
- `多相机操作任务`
- `语言条件机器人控制`
- `仿真到真实的实验迭代`
- `机器人数据集平台建设`
- `研究论文复现与 baseline 对比`

不太适合的场景也要说清楚：
- `硬实时工业控制`
- `高度安全关键、认证严格的生产环境`
- `以传统运动规划/模型预测控制为主、几乎不做学习的系统`
它不是 ROS2 的直接替代品，也不是工业 PLC 系统。

## 三、它的设计思想是什么
LeRobot 背后其实有一套很清晰的哲学。

1. `真实世界优先，不是只做 benchmark`
论文摘要明确强调它要打通从电机通信到大规模数据集和流式推理的全栈，并聚焦真实机器人，而不是只在模拟器里跑分。

2. `数据优先`
LeRobot 的中心对象不是单个模型，而是 `LeRobotDataset`。这和很多“论文代码仓库”不同。它默认你会不断采数据、修数据、共享数据、再训练。

3. `统一抽象，而不是每种硬件/算法各写一套脚本`
`Robot`、`Policy`、`Processor`、`Env`、`Dataset` 这些对象构成稳定接口。这样不同策略和硬件可以复用同一套工作流。

4. `artifact-first，而不是 notebook-first`
LeRobot 当然有 notebook，但真正重要的是：模型权重、配置、processor、数据集、metadata 都是显式 artifact，可以保存、加载、上传 Hub、版本化。这很利于复现。

5. `Hub-native`
它把 Hugging Face Hub 当成机器人学习的内容分发层。这种思路和传统机器人代码库很不一样，后者更偏“本地工程”，而 LeRobot 更偏“开放模型/开放数据生态”。

6. `强调可扩展的学习方法`
LeRobot 论文里有一句很重要：它偏向“随数据和算力扩展而提升的学习式方法”，而不是过度依赖手工规则。这解释了为什么它会同时拥抱 ACT、Diffusion、VLA、async inference 这些路线。

## 四、算法视角下，LeRobot 在做什么
如果把 LeRobot 放回机器人学习发展脉络，它大概站在这几条线的交汇处。

1. `ACT 代表低数据 imitation learning`
官方文档把 ACT 明确写成“最推荐的新手起点”。ACT 的思想是预测一段未来动作块，而不是一次只预测一步动作。它的优势是降低长时序误差累积，对精细操作很友好，数据量要求也低。ACT 对 LeRobot 的意义是：给真实机器人学习提供了一个轻量、可跑、可复现的强 baseline。

2. `Diffusion Policy 代表更强的动作分布建模`
Diffusion Policy 把动作生成看成条件扩散过程，能更好表示多模态动作分布，尤其适合“同样观测下有多种合理动作”的任务。它一般更重，但常常更稳、更强。LeRobot 支持它，说明框架不是只为一个轻模型服务，而是设计成能承载更通用的 modern policy family。

3. `SmolVLA 代表从 imitation 走向 foundation model`
SmolVLA 是 Hugging Face 自己推动的一条线：用公开社区数据训练一个轻量 VLA，让“语言 + 图像 +状态 -> 动作”成为统一范式。它背后的思想是，未来机器人不会只有 task-specific policy，而会越来越像多模态通用模型。SmolVLA 博客特别强调三点：`450M 小模型`、`社区共享数据`、`异步推理`。这三点正好体现 LeRobot 的整体方向：开放、可负担、可部署。

4. `RL / HIL / EnvHub 代表未来扩展`
LeRobot 虽然起家强项明显是 imitation learning + real robot data，但文档和代码已经扩展到强化学习、环境 Hub、IsaacLab Arena 等。说明它的 ambition 不只是“示教训练工具”，而是想成为一个更通用的 robot learning platform。

## 五、代码架构怎么理解
最好的理解方式，不是按目录背树，而是沿着一条主链路看：

`CLI -> Typed Config -> Factory -> Processor -> Dataset/Policy/Robot -> Hub`

先看 CLI 入口。`pyproject.toml` 直接把完整工作流注册成命令行工具：

```213:228:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/pyproject.toml
[project.scripts]
lerobot-calibrate="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_calibrate:main"
lerobot-find-cameras="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_find_cameras:main"
lerobot-find-port="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_find_port:main"
lerobot-record="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_record:main"
lerobot-replay="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_replay:main"
lerobot-setup-motors="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_setup_motors:main"
lerobot-teleoperate="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_teleoperate:main"
lerobot-eval="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_eval:main"
lerobot-train="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_train:main"
lerobot-train-tokenizer="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_train_tokenizer:main"
lerobot-dataset-viz="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_dataset_viz:main"
lerobot-info="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_info:main"
lerobot-find-joint-limits="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_find_joint_limits:main"
lerobot-imgtransform-viz="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_imgtransform_viz:main"
lerobot-edit-dataset="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_edit_dataset:main"
lerobot-setup-can="lerobot.scripts.lerobot_setup_can:main"
```

这说明 LeRobot 不是“一个模型包”，而是一整套操作系统式工作流。

然后看配置层。`configs/parser.py` 不是普通 CLI 解析器，它把 `draccus` typed config、Hub 上的预训练配置、插件发现整合在一起：

```195:233:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/src/lerobot/configs/parser.py
def wrap(config_path: Path | None = None) -> Callable[[F], F]:
    """
    HACK: Similar to draccus.wrap but does three additional things:
        - Will remove '.path' arguments from CLI in order to process them later on.
        - If a 'config_path' is passed and the main config class has a 'from_pretrained' method, will
          initialize it from there to allow to fetch configs from the hub directly
        - Will load plugins specified in the CLI arguments. These plugins will typically register
            their own subclasses of config classes, so that draccus can find the right class to instantiate
            from the CLI '.type' arguments
    """
    ...
                if has_method(argtype, "from_pretrained") and config_path_cli:
                    cli_args = filter_arg("config_path", cli_args)
                    cfg = argtype.from_pretrained(config_path_cli, cli_args=cli_args)
                else:
                    cfg = draccus.parse(config_class=argtype, config_path=config_path, args=cli_args)
```

这一步体现了它的一个核心思想：`配置本身也是可共享 artifact`。

再看数据层。`LeRobotDataset` 把数据集设计成“元信息 + parquet + video”的标准包：

```607:664:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/src/lerobot/datasets/lerobot_dataset.py
        In terms of files, LeRobotDataset encapsulates 3 main things:
            - metadata:
                - info contains various information about the dataset like shapes, keys, fps etc.
                - stats stores the dataset statistics of the different modalities for normalization
                - tasks contains the prompts for each task of the dataset, which can be used for
                  task-conditioned training.
            - hf_dataset (from datasets.Dataset), which will read any values from parquet files.
            - videos (optional) from which frames are loaded to be synchronous with data from parquet files.

        A typical LeRobotDataset looks like this from its root path:
        .
        ├── data
        │   ├── chunk-000
        │   │   ├── file-000.parquet
...
        ├── meta
        │   ├── episodes
        │   ├── info.json
        │   ├── stats.json
        │   └── tasks.parquet
        └── videos
            ├── observation.images.laptop
            │   ├── chunk-000
            │   │   ├── file-000.mp4
...
```

这套格式的优点是非常明显的：`可读`、`可流式`、`可上传`、`可视化`、`适合大规模数据管理`。

再看模型工厂。`policies/factory.py` 的关键不是“实例化某个类”，而是先从 `dataset.meta.features` 或 `env_cfg` 推断输入输出空间，再去造模型：

```405:471:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/src/lerobot/policies/factory.py
def make_policy(
    cfg: PreTrainedConfig,
    ds_meta: LeRobotDatasetMetadata | None = None,
    env_cfg: EnvConfig | None = None,
    rename_map: dict[str, str] | None = None,
) -> PreTrainedPolicy:
    ...
    if ds_meta is not None:
        features = dataset_to_policy_features(ds_meta.features)
    else:
        ...
        features = env_to_policy_features(env_cfg)

    cfg.output_features = {key: ft for key, ft in features.items() if ft.type is FeatureType.ACTION}
    if not cfg.input_features:
        cfg.input_features = {key: ft for key, ft in features.items() if key not in cfg.output_features}
```

这说明 LeRobot 的策略接口不是写死的，而是“由数据契约驱动”的。

最后看采集主流程。`lerobot_record.py` 真正把机器人、teleop、policy、processor、dataset 连在一起：

```446:518:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_record.py
    robot = make_robot_from_config(cfg.robot)
    teleop = make_teleoperator_from_config(cfg.teleop) if cfg.teleop is not None else None

    teleop_action_processor, robot_action_processor, robot_observation_processor = make_default_processors()

    dataset_features = combine_feature_dicts(
        aggregate_pipeline_dataset_features(
            pipeline=teleop_action_processor,
            initial_features=create_initial_features(
                action=robot.action_features
            ),
            use_videos=cfg.dataset.video,
        ),
        aggregate_pipeline_dataset_features(
            pipeline=robot_observation_processor,
            initial_features=create_initial_features(observation=robot.observation_features),
            use_videos=cfg.dataset.video,
        ),
    )
    ...
            dataset = LeRobotDataset.create(
                cfg.dataset.repo_id,
                cfg.dataset.fps,
                root=cfg.dataset.root,
                robot_type=robot.name,
                features=dataset_features,
                use_videos=cfg.dataset.video,
```

它后面再接上模型和 processor：

```506:581:/home/luogang/SRC/Robot/lerobot/src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_record.py
        policy = None if cfg.policy is None else make_policy(cfg.policy, ds_meta=dataset.meta)
        preprocessor = None
        postprocessor = None
        if cfg.policy is not None:
            preprocessor, postprocessor = make_pre_post_processors(
                policy_cfg=cfg.policy,
                pretrained_path=cfg.policy.pretrained_path,
                dataset_stats=rename_stats(dataset.meta.stats, cfg.dataset.rename_map),
                preprocessor_overrides={
                    "device_processor": {"device": cfg.policy.device},
                    "rename_observations_processor": {"rename_map": cfg.dataset.rename_map},
                },
            )
        ...
        with VideoEncodingManager(dataset):
            recorded_episodes = 0
            while recorded_episodes < cfg.dataset.num_episodes and not events["stop_recording"]:
                ...
                record_loop(
                    robot=robot,
                    ...
                    teleop=teleop,
                    policy=policy,
                    preprocessor=preprocessor,
                    postprocessor=postprocessor,
                    dataset=dataset,
```

所以 LeRobot 的架构不是“脚本堆起来”，而是围绕几个核心对象组织的：
- `Robot`
- `LeRobotDataset`
- `PreTrainedPolicy`
- `ProcessorPipeline`
- `Env`
- `Hub artifact`

`processor/pipeline.py` 和 `policies/pretrained.py` 进一步强化了这种思路：processor 可以序列化并上 Hub，policy 也有统一的 `from_pretrained / save / push to hub` 接口。这就是它为什么能把训练、评估、部署串起来。

## 六、这种实现方式的优点
1. `统一性强`
同一套抽象同时覆盖真实机器人、仿真、数据集、训练和部署，减少了机器人学习里常见的脚本碎片化。

2. `复现与协作成本低`
Hub-native 设计让模型、数据、配置、processor 都能共享和版本化，团队协作比传统“发个 zip 包”强很多。

3. `非常适合 imitation learning 工作流`
现实里很多机器人项目第一步不是 RL，而是示教采集和行为克隆。LeRobot 对这一主线支持非常完整。

4. `对低成本平台友好`
官方长期围绕 SO-100/SO-101、Koch、LeKiwi 等平台建设，能让更多人进入真实机器人学习，而不是只能用昂贵平台。

5. `算法可插拔`
ACT、Diffusion、SmolVLA、Pi0.5、GR00T 等共用统一壳层，这对研究者做 baseline 对比特别方便。

6. `异步推理很实用`
对于 action chunking / VLA 这类模型，推理耗时常是实际部署瓶颈。async inference 能明显改善机器人“停一下等推理”的问题。

7. `数据工程意识很强`
LeRobot 把数据格式、统计量、视频同步、任务描述、可视化都做成标准工具，这一点比很多论文仓库成熟。

## 七、这种实现方式的缺点和边界
1. `抽象层很多，学习曲线并不低`
它的目标是“降低机器人学习门槛”，但对完全没有 Python/深度学习/机器人经验的人来说，`config + factory + processor + Hub + dataset schema` 仍然不轻。

2. `对真实硬件的一致性是“抽象一致”，不是“物理一致”`
接口统一了，但不同机器人在控制频率、延迟、精度、相机布局、标定误差上的差异并不会消失。换句话说，LeRobot 统一的是软件入口，不是物理世界本身。

3. `更偏研究平台，不是工业级控制平台`
从 `Python`、`PyTorch`、`gRPC async inference`、`MP4/Parquet` 这些技术选择就能看出，它非常适合研究和快速迭代，但不适合硬实时、强安全约束的工业生产系统。

4. `生态发展很快，稳定性和一致性会有波动`
`pyproject.toml` 里项目状态还是 `Alpha`。再加上官方文档里稳定版和 `main` 分支内容差异不小，说明它还处在快速演进阶段。

5. `imitation learning 仍然是最顺滑主线`
虽然它支持 RL 和更大模型，但目前最成熟、最低摩擦的路径仍然是“录示教 -> 训 policy -> 上机器人”。如果你要做大规模在线 RL、复杂规划、多机器人系统，它未必是最合适的主框架。

6. `标准化带来约束`
统一数据格式和统一接口非常有价值，但对一些特殊传感器、复杂接触状态、力控、触觉、多机器人协同等任务，往往还需要额外扩展。

7. `对高质量数据依赖很强`
SmolVLA 文档和博客都反复强调，50 个 episode 往往只是起点，而且数据覆盖的 variation 很关键。也就是说，LeRobot 虽然降低了工具门槛，但并没有消除机器人学习最核心的瓶颈：高质量真实数据仍然昂贵。

## 八、如果你只记住一个总结
LeRobot 最本质的价值，不是“它实现了 ACT/SmolVLA”，而是它把机器人学习重新组织成了一个开放、标准化、可共享、可复现的工程体系。

它最擅长的事情是：
- 用统一接口接真实机器人
- 录制和管理示教数据
- 训练 imitation policy 或轻量 VLA
- 在仿真和真实世界做闭环评估
- 把模型和数据沉淀为可共享资产

它的核心竞争力是：
- `真实世界导向`
- `数据集优先`
- `Hub 原生协作`
- `统一抽象 + 可插拔算法`
- `对低成本硬件友好`

它的主要代价是：
- `抽象复杂度`
- `研究平台而非工业平台`
- `生态快速演进带来的不稳定`
- `真实机器人问题仍然绕不开数据质量和硬件差异`

2. `面向源码阅读的架构导图`

---

_**User**_

两种版本都整理一下给我吧

---

_**Agent (model gpt-5.4-medium, mode Agent)**_

我把上一轮的内容压缩成两套可直接使用的框架：一套偏“入门学习路径”，一套偏“源码/架构阅读路线”。## 版本一：面向初学者的学习路线图
这套版本的目标是让你从“完全理解 LeRobot 是什么”，逐步走到“能自己用它完成一个小项目”。你可以把它当成课程式学习地图。

### 0. 先建立总认识
先不要急着看模型细节，先把 LeRobot 理解成一句话：

`LeRobot = 真实机器人学习的一站式开源框架`

它主要解决 5 个问题：
- 怎么把真实机器人接进来
- 怎么录制标准化数据集
- 怎么训练策略
- 怎么评估策略
- 怎么把数据和模型分享到 Hub

如果只记住这一句，也可以：

`LeRobot 的核心不是某一个模型，而是一条从“示教数据”到“机器人执行”的统一工作流。`

---

### 1. 第一阶段：理解 LeRobot 的整体版图
这一阶段只回答“它是什么、能干什么、为什么存在”。

#### 你需要掌握的核心概念
- `Robot`：真实机器人统一接口
- `Teleoperator`：遥操作设备，如 leader arm、手柄、键盘、手机
- `LeRobotDataset`：标准数据集格式
- `Policy`：策略模型，如 ACT、Diffusion、SmolVLA
- `Processor`：输入输出变换流水线
- `Env`：仿真环境
- `Hub`：模型和数据的共享中心

#### 你应该形成的脑图
可以把 LeRobot 画成这样：

`硬件/相机/遥操作 -> 数据采集 -> 标准数据集 -> 策略训练 -> 仿真/真实评估 -> 部署运行 -> 上传共享`

#### 这一阶段建议重点读
- 官方首页
- `README.md`
- 文档首页 `index.mdx`
- 安装文档
- 硬件总览文档
- 数据集总览文档

#### 学完后你应该会回答的问题
- LeRobot 和普通论文代码有什么区别？
- 它为什么反复强调 Hub 和 Dataset？
- 它为什么特别适合真实机器人 imitation learning？

---

### 2. 第二阶段：理解它最适合什么场景
LeRobot 不是万能工具，它有非常明确的甜蜜区。

#### 最适合的场景
- `低成本机械臂示教学习`
- `桌面抓取/放置/装配`
- `多相机视觉操作`
- `从几十条示教开始训练策略`
- `研究型项目和复现型项目`
- `教学、hackathon、实验室原型系统`

#### 典型任务
- 抓积木放进盒子
- 拿起物体并放到指定区域
- 打开盒盖、插入部件
- 语言条件 pick-and-place
- 双臂精细操作

#### 不太适合的场景
- 强实时工业控制
- 高安全等级生产线
- 完全依赖传统控制和运动规划的系统
- 对 ROS/工业总线/实时性要求极强的系统

#### 这一阶段的核心认知
LeRobot 更像“机器人学习平台”，不是“工业机器人中控系统”。

---

### 3. 第三阶段：先学最核心的数据观
如果你想真正理解 LeRobot，最重要的是先理解它为什么把 `LeRobotDataset` 放在中心。

#### 为什么数据是核心
在机器人学习里，最难的通常不是“模型代码”，而是：
- 数据怎么录
- 图像和动作怎么同步
- 任务描述怎么保存
- 统计量怎么管理
- 数据怎么共享和复现

LeRobot 的回答是：把这些全部统一进一个标准格式。

#### 你要掌握的数据结构观
一个 LeRobot 数据集通常包含：
- `data/`：状态、动作等 parquet
- `meta/`：info、stats、tasks
- `videos/`：视频文件或图像

这意味着数据集不只是样本，而是一个完整资产：
- 可训练
- 可回放
- 可可视化
- 可上传
- 可流式读取

#### 初学者要理解的关键点
- `stats` 很重要，因为训练和推理常依赖归一化
- `tasks` 很重要，因为 VLA / 任务条件模型需要任务文本
- 视频和 parquet 同步是机器人数据工程的难点之一
- LeRobotDataset 让录制、训练、评估共享同一中间格式

#### 学习建议
先把数据集文档真正看懂，再去看模型。  
因为 LeRobot 的很多设计，都是围绕数据格式展开的。

---

### 4. 第四阶段：理解三类代表性模型
入门时不要被模型列表吓到，先抓主线。

#### 第一条主线：`ACT`
适合入门。
关键词：
- 轻量
- 数据效率高
- 适合精细操作
- 训练快

你可以把 ACT 理解成：
`用 Transformer 一次预测一小段未来动作，而不是一步一步地预测。`

它为什么重要：
- 减少长时序误差积累
- 对接触丰富的操作任务很友好
- 对小数据场景效果常常很好

#### 第二条主线：`Diffusion Policy`
适合理解现代机器人策略建模。
关键词：
- 动作分布建模能力更强
- 更擅长处理多模态动作
- 推理和训练更重

你可以把它理解成：
`不是直接给动作，而是从噪声中逐步“生成”合理动作序列。`

#### 第三条主线：`SmolVLA`
适合理解 LeRobot 的未来方向。
关键词：
- 视觉 + 语言 + 动作统一
- foundation model 路线
- 轻量化 VLA
- 社区数据驱动

你可以把它理解成：
`让机器人不只看图像和状态，还能理解语言任务描述，再输出动作。`

#### 初学者推荐顺序
1. 先理解 `ACT`
2. 再理解 `Diffusion Policy`
3. 最后理解 `SmolVLA`

这样最自然，因为它正好对应：
`传统 imitation -> 更强生成式策略 -> 通用多模态机器人模型`

---

### 5. 第五阶段：学会 LeRobot 的标准工作流
这一阶段最重要，因为它决定你是否真的“会用”。

#### 标准工作流
1. 接机器人和相机
2. 用 teleop 录制示教
3. 生成 `LeRobotDataset`
4. 训练策略
5. 在仿真或真实机器人上评估
6. 根据失败案例继续补数据、再训练

#### 你应该重点理解的命令
- `lerobot-record`
- `lerobot-replay`
- `lerobot-train`
- `lerobot-eval`
- `lerobot-teleoperate`
- `lerobot-dataset-viz`

#### 这条工作流的本质
LeRobot 并不是把“采集”和“部署”分成完全不同系统。  
它更像一个统一控制框架，其中：
- teleop 是一种 action source
- policy 也是一种 action source
- dataset 是两者共享的记录层

这就是为什么它特别适合快速实验迭代。

---

### 6. 第六阶段：理解它的设计思想
这一阶段开始从“会用”走向“看懂为什么这样设计”。

#### 设计思想一：`真实世界优先`
很多 ML 框架主要为 benchmark 服务，LeRobot 从一开始就想支持真实机器人。

#### 设计思想二：`数据集优先`
不是先把模型做复杂，而是先把数据工作流统一。

#### 设计思想三：`统一抽象`
`Robot / Policy / Dataset / Processor / Env` 构成稳定接口。

#### 设计思想四：`Hub-native`
模型、数据、配置、processor 都是可共享 artifact。

#### 设计思想五：`可扩展的学习范式`
LeRobot 倾向那些能随着数据和算力增长持续变强的方法，而不是 heavily hand-engineered pipeline。

---

### 7. 初学者的最短实践路线
如果你想最快建立实感，我建议：

1. 先只看 `README` 和官方文档首页
2. 看 `ACT` 文档，理解 action chunking
3. 看 `LeRobotDataset` 文档，理解数据格式
4. 看 `record -> train -> eval` 这条链路
5. 再去看 `SmolVLA`，理解 LeRobot 的未来方向

如果你有硬件，最佳路径是：
1. 录一个很小的数据集
2. 训练一个 ACT
3. 真机跑起来
4. 再去尝试 SmolVLA

---

## 版本二：面向源码阅读的架构导图
这套版本适合你要真正“吃透代码架构”，理解 LeRobot 是如何把这些概念落地的。

### 总体阅读原则
不要按目录机械地从头翻到尾。  
最好的阅读主线是：

`CLI -> Config -> Factory -> Processor -> Dataset/Policy/Robot -> Hub`

因为这正是 LeRobot 运行时的数据和控制流。

---

### 1. 先看顶层入口，建立工程地图
先看两个文件：
- `README.md`
- `pyproject.toml`

这里能建立全局视角。

#### 你要关注什么
- 项目对自己的定位
- 暴露了哪些 CLI
- optional dependencies 怎么组织
- 哪些能力是核心能力，哪些是扩展能力

#### 为什么先看这里
因为 LeRobot 是典型的“工作流型框架”，不是单一算法包。  
CLI 入口基本就定义了它的产品形态。

---

### 2. 再看配置系统，这是整个框架的中枢
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/configs/parser.py`
- `src/lerobot/configs/default.py`
- `src/lerobot/configs/train.py`
- `src/lerobot/configs/eval.py`
- 以及各模块 `config_*.py`

#### 你要理解的重点
- 它为什么用 typed config
- CLI 参数如何映射到 dataclass/config 对象
- `from_pretrained` 如何把 Hub 上的配置拉下来
- 插件机制如何让第三方 policy/robot 挂进来

#### 读懂后的关键结论
LeRobot 的配置层不是“参数解析器”，而是：
`本地运行配置 + 远程预训练配置 + 插件注册系统` 的统一入口。

这一步很重要，因为后面的 factory 全都依赖 config。

---

### 3. 然后看工厂模式，理解“对象是怎么被造出来的”
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/policies/factory.py`
- `src/lerobot/envs/factory.py`
- `src/lerobot/datasets/factory.py`
- `src/lerobot/robots/utils.py`
- `src/lerobot/teleoperators/utils.py`

#### 为什么 factory 很关键
LeRobot 最大的工程特点之一是：
`所有主对象都不是在脚本里手写 new，而是由配置驱动工厂创建。`

#### 你要关注的问题
- policy 的输入输出特征从哪来
- dataset metadata 如何影响模型配置
- env feature 和 dataset feature 如何统一
- robot / teleoperator 是怎么按 config 实例化的

#### 最关键的认知
LeRobot 真正的“统一性”并不只是抽象类，而是：
`工厂函数 + feature schema + config system`  
三者一起保证了模块可组合。

---

### 4. 再看数据集实现，这是全框架的地基
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/datasets/lerobot_dataset.py`
- `src/lerobot/datasets/dataset_tools.py`
- `src/lerobot/datasets/utils.py`

#### 这里最重要的阅读目标
不是看每个函数，而是回答这几个问题：
- 数据集的物理组织是什么
- metadata 存什么
- stats/task/video 为什么和主数据分开
- 为什么用 parquet + mp4
- 如何支持 streaming / Hub / visualization

#### 你应该抓住的核心思想
LeRobotDataset 是整个框架的“中间格式”和“资产格式”。

它解决的是：
- 采集时怎么存
- 训练时怎么读
- 回放时怎么重建动作
- 分享时怎么上传
- 可视化时怎么同步视频与状态

#### 阅读建议
先看类说明和整体结构，再看 create/load/save/push 的主流程，不要一开始陷在编码细节里。

---

### 5. 然后看硬件抽象，理解真实机器人是怎么统一的
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/robots/robot.py`
- `src/lerobot/teleoperators/teleoperator.py`
- `src/lerobot/cameras/*`
- `src/lerobot/motors/*`

#### 你要重点理解
- `Robot` 的 observation/action 契约
- connect/calibrate/configure/disconnect 生命周期
- 机器人类怎样暴露 feature schema
- teleoperator 为什么也是独立抽象

#### 这里的阅读结论
LeRobot 统一真实硬件的关键不是“统一通信协议”，而是：
`统一 observation/action 的软件语义接口`

这意味着硬件差异没有消失，但上层训练和数据流程可以复用。

---

### 6. 再看 processor，这是 LeRobot 最体现工程成熟度的一层
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/processor/pipeline.py`
- `src/lerobot/processor/core.py`
- 相关 processor 文档

#### 为什么这一层特别重要
很多框架把归一化、字段改名、环境适配、设备适配混进模型或脚本里。  
LeRobot 把它们抽成独立 pipeline，这是它能统一不同来源数据和不同部署场景的关键。

#### 你要重点理解
- `ProcessorStepRegistry`
- `DataProcessorPipeline`
- preprocessor / postprocessor 的职责边界
- feature transform 是怎么跟踪的
- pipeline 如何保存/加载/上 Hub

#### 读懂后的核心结论
LeRobot 的可迁移性，很大程度不是来自某个万能模型，而是来自：
`processor 把不同系统之间的数据接口缝合起来。`

---

### 7. 然后看 policy 基类和各算法实现
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/policies/pretrained.py`
- `src/lerobot/policies/factory.py`
- `src/lerobot/policies/act/*`
- `src/lerobot/policies/diffusion/*`
- `src/lerobot/policies/smolvla/*`
- `src/lerobot/policies/pi05/*`
- `src/lerobot/policies/groot/*`

#### 推荐阅读顺序
1. 先看 `pretrained.py`
2. 再看 `factory.py`
3. 然后挑一个最熟悉的算法细看，比如 `act`
4. 最后再看 `smolvla`

#### 你要抓住的接口层
- `from_pretrained`
- `forward`
- `predict_action_chunk`
- `select_action`
- `reset`
- `push_model_to_hub`

#### 这里的关键结论
LeRobot 不是把算法作为孤立实现放进去，而是强制它们满足统一的部署/训练契约。  
这也是为什么 action-chunk 模型、diffusion 模型、VLA 模型都能挂进同一工作流。

---

### 8. 再看四个关键脚本，理解主流程怎么闭环
这是源码阅读最值得精读的一组文件：
- `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_record.py`
- `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_replay.py`
- `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_train.py`
- `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_eval.py`

#### 四个脚本各自回答的问题
- `record`：数据是怎么从机器人流入 dataset 的
- `replay`：dataset 的 action 是怎么重新回到机器人上的
- `train`：dataset/meta/stats 是怎么进入 policy 的
- `eval`：policy 是怎么和 env 做 rollout 的

#### 推荐重点读法
先精读 `lerobot_record.py`。  
因为它最能体现 LeRobot 的全栈统一思想：
- robot
- teleop
- policy
- processor
- dataset
- video encoding
- episode lifecycle

然后看 `lerobot_eval.py`，理解仿真评估如何复用同一套 processor/policy 契约。

---

### 9. 读异步推理模块，理解它的部署特色
重点文件：
- `src/lerobot/async_inference/policy_server.py`
- `src/lerobot/async_inference/robot_client.py`

#### 为什么这部分值得看
这不是一个“可有可无的小优化”，而是 LeRobot 对现代 action-chunk / VLA 模型部署问题的一个很有代表性的回答。

#### 你要关注什么
- 为什么 observation queue 只保留最新观测
- 策略如何在远程 GPU 上加载
- pre/post processors 怎样跟随部署一起加载
- 动作块是如何异步返回的
- 为什么 async 能比 sync 更灵活

#### 架构层面的意义
这说明 LeRobot 不只关心“训练得出来”，还关心“真实机器人跑的时候不要卡顿”。

---

### 10. 最后看文档和 examples，反向验证你的理解
重点目录：
- `docs/source/`
- `examples/`

#### 为什么最后看它们
因为到这一步你已经有架构认知了，再看文档和示例，能把“抽象设计”映射回“用户使用路径”。

#### 重点建议
- 用 `examples/tutorial/act`
- `examples/tutorial/diffusion`
- `examples/tutorial/smolvla`
- `examples/tutorial/async-inf`
来对照前面源码理解

这样你会发现：
文档写的是“怎么用”，源码体现的是“为什么能这样用”。

---

## 一份可执行的源码阅读顺序
如果你要真正读一遍，我建议按这个顺序：

1. `README.md`
2. `pyproject.toml`
3. `src/lerobot/configs/parser.py`
4. `src/lerobot/robots/robot.py`
5. `src/lerobot/datasets/lerobot_dataset.py`
6. `src/lerobot/processor/pipeline.py`
7. `src/lerobot/policies/pretrained.py`
8. `src/lerobot/policies/factory.py`
9. `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_record.py`
10. `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_train.py`
11. `src/lerobot/scripts/lerobot_eval.py`
12. `src/lerobot/async_inference/policy_server.py`
13. 对应 `docs/source/*.mdx`
14. 对应 `examples/*`

这个顺序的好处是：  
你会先抓住框架骨架，再去看局部模型细节，而不是一开始就掉进某个 policy 文件里。

---

## 两套版本怎么配合使用
最好的用法不是二选一，而是配合起来。

如果你是：
- `刚入门`：先看“初学者路线图”
- `要写综述/教程`：先按“初学者路线图”组织内容，再用“架构导图”补充代码依据
- `要改源码/做二次开发`：直接按“架构导图”读
- `要训自己的机器人任务`：先走“初学者路线图”的工作流，再查“架构导图”的实现细节

你可以把它们理解成：
- `初学者版` 解决“知道是什么、怎么学”
- `源码版` 解决“知道为什么这样设计、代码怎么运转”

如果你愿意，我下一步可以继续帮你做两种补充之一：
1. 把这两套内容整理成一篇 `结构化 Markdown 文档`
2. 结合你现在打开的 `docs/bt/ov/lerobot050doc_note.md`，直接续写成你仓库里的正式笔记稿
